# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **File I/O**
+ **Sequential Reads and Writes**
+ **Stream State**
:::

::: notes
Recall that file streams use the same insertion and extraction operators as cin and cout. Sequential operations automatically advance the stream position.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- Stringstream
- Random Access Files

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_stringstream.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_random_access_files.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

+ String streams parse and format text using familiar stream operators
+ Stream state reports whether typed extraction succeeded
+ Random access moves directly to a calculated byte position
+ Fixed-size binary values make index-based offsets predictable

<!-- Slide 37 -->

---

# Next Time

<!-- Slide 38 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 39 -->

---

## Stringstream: Good Practices

+ Use `istringstream` for parsing and `ostringstream` for formatting
+ Check stream state before using extracted values
+ Keep input collection separate from interpretation

<!-- Slide 40 -->

---

## Stringstream: Common Mistakes

+ Assuming extraction succeeded without checking the stream
+ Expecting `>>` to preserve spaces inside a field
+ Treating a simple delimiter split as a complete CSV parser

<!-- Slide 41 -->

---

## Stringstream: More Examples

```{.cpp}
string text = "18 72.5 true";
istringstream input(text);

int age;
double weight;
bool active;

input >> age >> weight >> boolalpha >> active;
```

<!-- Slide 42 -->

---

## Stringstream: Practice Problems

1. Parse a line containing a product name, quantity, and price.
2. Build a receipt line with a fixed two-decimal total.
3. Split a pipe-delimited record into three fields.

<!-- Slide 43 -->

---

## Stringstream: AI Search Terms

+ C++ `istringstream` parse typed values
+ C++ `ostringstream` build formatted string
+ C++ `getline` custom delimiter stringstream
+ C++ check string stream extraction failure

<!-- Slide 44 -->

---

## Stringstream: Questions for Reflection

+ Why separate reading a complete line from parsing its values?
+ When is `getline()` more appropriate than `>>`?
+ What information does stream state provide?

<!-- Slide 45 -->

---

## Random Access Files: Good Practices

+ Open random-access data with `ios::binary`
+ Calculate offsets with `sizeof` rather than a hardcoded width
+ Validate the requested index and check every file operation

<!-- Slide 46 -->

---

## Random Access Files: Common Mistakes

+ Using `seekg()` before a write or `seekp()` before a read
+ Calculating offsets for variable-length text records
+ Opening an update stream in a mode that truncates the file

<!-- Slide 47 -->

---

## Random Access Files: More Examples

Read the final stored `double`:

```{.cpp}
input.seekg(-static_cast<streamoff>(sizeof(double)), ios::end);

double lastScore;
input.read(reinterpret_cast<char*>(&lastScore),
           sizeof(lastScore));
```

<!-- Slide 48 -->

---

## Random Access Files: Practice Problems

1. Determine how many `int` values are stored in a binary file.
2. Read the value at an index supplied by the user.
3. Replace the final `double` without changing earlier values.

<!-- Slide 49 -->

---

## Random Access Files: AI Search Terms

+ C++ binary file `seekg` and `seekp`
+ C++ `tellg` determine binary file size
+ C++ random access fixed-size values
+ C++ `fstream` update binary file without truncating

<!-- Slide 50 -->

---

## Random Access Files: Questions for Reflection

+ Why must values have a fixed width for index-based offsets?
+ What is the difference between the get and put positions?
+ When is sequential text preferable to random-access binary data?

<!-- Slide 51 -->
